title: Adult Content Subscription Card Validation Before High-Value Fraud
narrative: Fraudster validates stolen card credentials using adult entertainment subscription services ($29.95-$39.95 monthly recurring charges) that typically have relaxed fraud controls and minimal cardholder scrutiny due to embarrassment factor. The attacker initiates 1-3 adult content subscriptions with ProductCD='W' (web services) within 2-hour window, often between 01:00-05:00 UTC when card1_txn_count_24hr is zero, establishing the card as 'live' without triggering velocity alerts. These validators use disposable email domains (P_emaildomain in guerrillamail.com, tempmail.net, 10minutemail.com) and residential proxies showing dist1 values 300-800km from card billing addr1. Within 18-36 hours post-validation, the same card1 attempts 3-8 high-value purchases ($800-$2400) at electronics or luxury goods MCCs. The adult subscriptions remain active 48-72 hours before chargeback, creating temporal separation from the primary fraud.
indicators: Initial transaction ProductCD='W' for adult content MCC (5967) ranging $29.95-$39.95
P_emaildomain matches disposable/temporary email provider list (10+ known domains)
card1_txn_count_24hr equals 0 at time of subscription, jumps to 1-3 within 120 minutes
txn_hour between 1-5 (UTC night hours) for initial validation transactions
dist1 between billing addr1 and transaction origin 300-800km indicating proxy use
Same card1 shows 3-8 transactions totaling $800-$2400 within 18-36 hours post-subscription
Time delta between first adult subscription and first high-value purchase 18-36 hours, card1_amt_zscore jumps from 0.2 to 3.5+
summary: A stolen card makes 1-3 small adult entertainment subscriptions ($30-40) in the early morning hours using temporary email addresses and a location 300-800km from the billing address, then attempts multiple high-value purchases ($800-$2400) 18-36 hours later.
reasoning: Fraudsters use adult content subscriptions to test stolen cards because victims rarely dispute these charges due to embarrassment, allowing the attacker to confirm the card works before making larger fraudulent purchases at electronics or luxury retailers.
indicator_explanations: Small adult site charge tests if the card works before larger fraud
Throwaway email address suggests fraudster avoiding identity trace
Card goes from completely dormant to multiple hits in two hours
Transactions happen in the dead of night when cardholder likely sleeps
Billing address is hundreds of miles from where transaction actually originates
Card explodes into a spending spree within a day of validation charge
Spending pattern rockets from normal baseline to extreme outlier overnight
